claims.text: Diet Coke is formulated to taste noticeably more bitter than regular Coke because consumers need a taste signal to believe the product is genuinely different; without this sensory distinction, they don't accept the diet positioning.
claims.speaker: Behavioral Science Consultant
claims.mechanism: Sensory difference signals product authenticity; taste difference creates belief in functional difference (diet efficacy)

claims.text: Medicinal products are deliberately formulated to taste unpleasant because unpleasant taste increases consumer belief in product efficacy; palatability reduction operates as a credibility signal.
claims.speaker: Behavioral Science Consultant
claims.mechanism: Unpleasant sensory experience signals potency; consumers infer: if it tastes bad, it must work
bounded_by: Does not apply to consumer-preference-driven categories where taste alone drives repeat purchase

claims.text: When applying behavioral science techniques to business problems, the ethical boundary is whether you would publicly present the tactic to an audience including journalists and the public without expecting widespread outrage; if significant numbers of people would be outraged upon discovering the hidden mechanism, the tactic is ethically problematic.
claims.speaker: Behavioral Science Consultant
conditions: Behavioral science application in commercial context
bounded_by: This is a personal ethical threshold, not a universal standard

claims.text: Large companies with established reputations avoid deceptive marketing tactics not because they are ethical in principle, but because the reputational risk of discovery outweighs any short-term benefit from the tactic; they rationally protect their brand equity.
claims.speaker: Behavioral Science Consultant
conditions: High public visibility and media scrutiny
bounded_by: Does not apply to startup or unproven brands where reputational capital is minimal
Does not apply to sectors with light regulatory oversight

practices.text: When designing product attributes (taste, texture, appearance), consider whether sensory signals will reinforce or contradict the positioning claim; mismatched signals reduce belief in the product benefit.
